title: Foundation of the Song dynasty by Zhao Kuangyin
summary: On the last day of the Chinese new year of 960, at a bridge outside the Later Zhou capital of Kaifeng, the general Zhao Kuangyin allowed his mutinous army to drape him in a yellow robe and proclaim him emperor. Within days he was on the throne as Taizu, founder of the Song dynasty. His reign ended the century of chaos that had followed the Tang collapse, disarmed the warlord culture that had produced it, and set China on a path of civil bureaucracy, commercial vitality and scientific creativity that would define the next three centuries.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Tang dynasty, once the great cosmopolitan power of East Asia, had finally expired in 907 when the warlord Zhu Wen deposed its last child emperor. In the north five successive short-lived dynasties, the Later Liang, Later Tang, Later Jin, Later Han and Later Zhou, replaced each other in Kaifeng and Luoyang in barely half a century, most of them founded by frontier military governors of Shatuo Turkic origin. In the south ten more or less independent kingdoms, from Wu-Yue on the lower Yangtze to Southern Han in Guangdong, ruled their own regional courts. The great cities of the north had been sacked repeatedly; the Grand Canal was silting; the classical educated elite was scattered. It was, in the memory of Song historians, a time of soldiers.

The strongest of the northern regimes was the last of the five, the Later Zhou, founded in 951 by the veteran general Guo Wei. His adopted son and successor Chai Rong, known posthumously as Emperor Shizong, was a man of unusual vigour who between 954 and 959 recovered strategic territory in Shanxi, retook the coastal prefectures of Ying and Mo from the Khitan Liao, and prepared to reconquer the sixteen prefectures around modern Beijing that Later Jin had ceded in 938. Shizong died suddenly on 27 July 959, aged only thirty-eight, leaving his six-year-old son Chai Zongxun as emperor.

The court that surrounded the child emperor was dominated by his mother Empress Dowager Fu and by the chief minister Fan Zhi. Their most formidable general was Zhao Kuangyin, a Hebei-born officer in his early thirties who had served Shizong loyally as commander of the Palace Corps, the elite metropolitan army. Zhao was popular with the troops, connected by sworn brotherhoods to a network of fellow officers, and known for a personal manner combining bluff comradeship with obvious command. He was almost certainly the most powerful individual in Kaifeng.

Rumours of a Khitan invasion of the north, together with a raid from Northern Han in Shanxi, gave the pretext for a mobilisation. In the last days of the first month of the year 960 by the Chinese calendar, corresponding to early February, Zhao was ordered north with the main field army. On the second night of the campaign, at the small garrison town of Chenqiao Yi some forty kilometres north-east of Kaifeng, his officers gathered around him and pressed the argument that the empire needed an adult ruler. There were reports of two suns fighting in the sky, always in Chinese tradition a sign of the transfer of the mandate.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On the morning of the third day at Chenqiao, a delegation of soldiers burst into Zhao Kuangyin's tent, threw a yellow robe over his shoulders and proclaimed him emperor. The yellow robe was the exclusive dress of the Chinese sovereign; putting it on was, in the language of ritual, the accomplished fact. Zhao, according to the version soon canonised by his own historians, made the customary ritual protests before consenting on strict conditions. He required his officers to swear that on entering Kaifeng they would neither harm the young emperor and his family nor sack the city; that they would obey his orders like their own limbs; and that any officer breaking these terms would be executed with all his household. On these terms the army marched south.

Kaifeng opened its gates without a fight on the morning of 4 February 960. The palace commander Han Tong, one of the few officials disposed to resist, was killed almost immediately in the streets. The Zhou chief minister Fan Zhi acceded to the change of dynasty on the promise of no reprisals. The child emperor Chai Zongxun was demoted to the rank of Prince of Zheng and, unusually for such transitions, allowed to live in comfortable custody. Zhao Kuangyin performed the ritual sacrifice at the ancestral altars on 5 February and took the throne. He chose Song as his dynastic title, from the ancient name of his own commandery, and Jianlong, meaning Establishment of Splendour, as his reign era. History would know him as Emperor Taizu, Grand Progenitor of Song.

Almost his first act was to secure the loyalty of the very military network that had raised him. In an event that entered Chinese folklore as "releasing military power over cups of wine", he invited his old comrades, above all Shi Shouxin, Wang Shenqi and Gao Huaide, to a private banquet in 961. According to the Continued Zizhi Tongjian and later chronicles, he thanked them tearfully, confessed he had not slept easy since accepting the throne, and asked them how he could rest when any of them might in turn find themselves draped in a yellow robe by their own soldiers. The generals took the point. They petitioned for retirement, received generous estates, wealthy marriages between their children and the imperial family, and honorific titles emptied of real authority. The Palace Corps was reorganised under officers of lower rank rotated through frequent transfers, ensuring no single figure could again build a personal army.

With his rear secure Taizu turned to reunification. The independent kingdoms of the south, wealthy but militarily weak, were absorbed in a series of well-planned campaigns. Jingnan and Hunan fell in 963, Later Shu in Sichuan in 965, Southern Han in Guangdong in 971, and the great state of Southern Tang, ruled from Nanjing by the poet-king Li Yu, in 976 after a long siege. Only Wu-Yue was allowed to accede peacefully in 978, after Taizu's death, and only the Khitan Liao in the north and the Tangut Xi Xia in the north-west remained beyond Song control. Taizu himself died suddenly on 14 November 976, aged forty-nine, in circumstances that later gossip attributed to his younger brother Zhao Kuangyi, who succeeded him as Emperor Taizong.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The political shape of Song China took form quickly. In place of the semi-autonomous jiedushi that had wrecked the late Tang, Taizu installed circuit intendants and prefects appointed and rotated by the central chancellery. Local military forces were reduced to weak garrisons; the strategic reserve was concentrated in the Palace Corps around Kaifeng. To pay this professional army the government relied on a highly monetised economy, with a state-issued copper coinage, salt and iron monopolies, and by the eleventh century the world's first paper money, the jiaozi, first issued in Sichuan.

Civil authority took clear precedence over the military. Taizu insisted that the highest posts of state go to scholars who had passed the imperial examinations. Under his brother and successor Taizong the number of examination graduates increased sharply, and by the end of the tenth century the exams had become the pre-eminent path to power, drawing thousands of candidates from the gentry of every province. The Song emerged over the next century as the most systematically bureaucratic and educated state in the world. Its senior officials, from the reformist chancellors Fan Zhongyan and Wang Anshi to the poet-statesman Su Shi, were public intellectuals as much as administrators, and Confucian philosophy would flower under their patronage in the neo-Confucian synthesis of Zhu Xi.

Economically the Song oversaw an unprecedented expansion. The wet-rice agriculture of the Yangtze basin, using new early-ripening Champa strains introduced from Vietnam under Taizong, doubled the empire's food supply and shifted population and wealth southward. Cities such as Kaifeng, and later Hangzhou after the loss of the north in 1127, grew to a size not seen anywhere in the world. Overseas trade with Southeast Asia, India and the Persian Gulf boomed; Chinese ships appeared regularly in Sri Lankan and East African ports. The state minted more coin annually than any previous or subsequent Chinese dynasty until the twentieth century.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The scientific and technological creativity of Song China is difficult to summarise briefly. Gunpowder, first attested in the mid-ninth century, became a military technology under the Song, deployed in flame throwers, incendiary bombs and by the twelfth century in true firearms. Movable-type printing was invented by the artisan Bi Sheng in Kaifeng around 1040. The magnetic compass, described in Shen Kuo's Mengxi Bitan of the 1080s, transformed navigation. Chinese shipwrights of the Song era built watertight bulkhead hulls, stern-post rudders and multiple-masted junks that outclassed anything in the medieval world. Song astronomers such as Su Song built the great water-driven astronomical clock tower at Kaifeng in 1092, complete with an escapement mechanism that anticipated European clockwork by two centuries.

Culturally the dynasty produced perhaps the greatest concentration of painters, poets and philosophers in premodern Chinese history. Landscape painters such as Fan Kuan, Guo Xi and later Ma Yuan and Xia Gui redefined the tradition; poets such as Su Shi, Ouyang Xiu and the woman writer Li Qingzhao gave the ci lyric a canonical form; historians such as Sima Guang wrote comprehensive chronicles that shaped later imperial historiography; philosophers such as Zhu Xi codified a neo-Confucianism that would remain the state orthodoxy of China, Korea, Vietnam and much of Japan into the twentieth century.

The Song polity itself did not last unaltered. In 1127 the Jurchen Jin captured Kaifeng and the two Song emperors were taken north into captivity, ending the Northern Song period. A branch of the imperial family re-established a Southern Song at Hangzhou, which ruled the Yangtze basin and the south until the Mongol conquest of 1279. Yet across the dynasty's three centuries the essential template laid down in 960 held: civil examination bureaucracy, professional army under central control, monetised commercial economy, patronage of learning. When later Chinese thought about what a functioning empire should look like, from the Ming founder Zhu Yuanzhang to the Qing reformers of the nineteenth century, they thought about Song. The morning at Chenqiao Yi when a yellow robe was placed on Zhao Kuangyin's shoulders proved to be one of the most consequential mornings in Chinese history.

worldContext.text: The Abbasid caliphate at Baghdad was under the effective control of the Iranian Buyid dynasty, whose emirs kept the caliph as a religious figurehead. In Egypt the Isma'ili Shi'ite Fatimids under al-Mu'izz were preparing the invasion that would take Cairo in 969 and establish a rival caliphate on the Nile. Byzantine emperors Nikephoros II Phokas and John I Tzimiskes were leading a spectacular military revival, retaking Crete in 961, Cyprus in 965 and Antioch in 969 from Islamic rulers.

In central Europe the Saxon king Otto I was crowned Holy Roman Emperor by Pope John XII in 962, reviving Charlemagne's imperial dignity. In England the reforming king Edgar the Peaceful ruled a united Anglo-Saxon kingdom, and in 973 was crowned emperor of Britain at Bath. The Viking rulers Harald Bluetooth of Denmark and Vladimir the Great of the Rus were on the eve of their conversions to Christianity, opening Scandinavia and Kievan Rus to Latin and Greek Christendom respectively.

In India the Chola dynasty of Tamil Nadu was rising to become the dominant power of the south under Parantaka I and shortly Rajaraja I, who would build the Brihadishvara temple at Thanjavur. The Rashtrakutas of the Deccan were in their last decade, soon to be replaced by the Later Chalukyas of Kalyani. In the north the Ghaznavid ruler Sabuktigin had just consolidated his power at Ghazni and was preparing the raids into India that his son Mahmud would make notorious.

In West Africa the Wagadu kingdom of Ghana was at its height, its capital Kumbi Saleh trading gold and salt through the Sanhaja Berber intermediaries of the Sahara. On the East African coast Swahili trading cities such as Mogadishu, Kilwa and Zanzibar were emerging as entrepots between the Indian Ocean and the interior. The Christian kingdoms of Nubia continued their long coexistence with Fatimid Egypt, and in the Ethiopian highlands the Zagwe dynasty was about to succeed the last Aksumite rulers.

In central Mexico the Toltec capital of Tula, in what is now Hidalgo, was consolidating its regional hegemony under kings later remembered in legend as Ce Acatl Topiltzin Quetzalcoatl. In the Yucatan peninsula the northern Maya cities of Chichen Itza and Uxmal were flourishing while the southern lowland cities lay abandoned. On the north coast of Peru the Chimu kingdom was expanding from its capital Chan Chan, and in the Andean altiplano regional cultures were emerging from the Tiwanaku collapse.

figures.name: Zhao Kuangyin (Emperor Taizu of Song)
figures.role: Founder of the Song dynasty; commander of the Palace Corps under Later Zhou; proclaimed emperor at Chenqiao in February 960; reigned until 976

figures.name: Chai Zongxun
figures.role: Child emperor of Later Zhou, deposed and demoted to Prince of Zheng in 960; allowed to live in comfortable retirement

figures.name: Zhao Kuangyi (Emperor Taizong of Song)
figures.role: Younger brother of Taizu; succeeded him as emperor in 976 and completed the reunification of most of Han China

figures.name: Shi Shouxin
figures.role: Sworn brother of Taizu and senior general; retired at the Cup of Wine banquet in 961 with land, title and imperial marriage alliance

figures.name: Fan Zhi
figures.role: Later Zhou chief minister who accepted the change of dynasty and continued to serve under Taizu as senior councillor

images.caption: Portrait of Emperor Taizu of Song, hanging scroll in ink and colours on silk, anonymous, mounted during the Qing dynasty. National Palace Museum, Taipei.
